metadata.title: Why Cancer Cells Run an Inefficient Engine on Purpose
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut — Candidate 01. The Warburg effect as carbon-allocation strategy: a dividing cell's primary need is not energy but carbon to build a second copy of itself.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = efficient/kept/built (carbon that goes to structure); CRIMSON #BF3339 = wasted/excreted/lost (lactate, CO2, the naive expectation); GOLD = editor's-pen highlighter only, never text; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO detailed TCA cycle chemistry, NO specific enzyme kinetics, NO HIF-1alpha or VHL discussion (separate cards), NO isotope-tracing experiments, NO Otto Warburg history beyond one sentence. Manim move: split (carbon fans into destinations). THE EXAMPLE act is mandatory before RECAP per the 16:9 full cut law.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Pragmatist register (voices/pragmatist/VOICE.md + HAI.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the HAI outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Humanitarians AI
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: On a PET scan, a cluster of lymph nodes blazes hot — consuming glucose at twenty times the rate of normal tissue. The puzzle is not the consumption rate. It is what the cells are doing with that sugar.

card.copy: Why Cancer Cells Run an Inefficient Engine on Purpose

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] eyebrow CANCER BIOLOGY · teal; title card

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] eyebrow CANCER BIOLOGY · teal; title card

beats.narration_text: The tumor is not burning that glucose for energy. Most converts to lactate and gets excreted as waste. A tumor in a well-oxygenated body, extracting two units of energy from a sugar when it could extract thirty.

production_viz.label: 2 ATP vs 30 ATP
production_viz.mechanic: two vertical bars rise from a zero baseline — a short CRIMSON bar (2 ATP, glycolysis to lactate) and a tall TEAL bar (30 ATP, complete oxidation); data labels on each bar
production_viz.note: the per-glucose efficiency gap — introduce the puzzle without explaining it yet

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] 2 vs 30 ATP bar chart — the apparent waste

beats.narration_text: A cancer cell is energy-hungry and dividing rapidly. It should maximize ATP per glucose. Instead: two ATP per glucose, the rest excreted as lactate. Why?

card.copy: A cancer cell should maximize ATP from glucose. Here is a tumor extracting only two — and excreting the rest as lactate. Why?

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] THE QUESTION — on screen and in narration

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] THE QUESTION — on screen and in narration

beats.narration_text: A quiescent cell — a resting neuron, a liver cell not under stress — has one metabolic job: make enough ATP to stay running. Complete oxidation to carbon dioxide is optimal: thirty ATP per molecule.

production_viz.label: quiescent cell: glucose goes to CO2
production_viz.mechanic: a glucose node on the left, an arrow leading rightward through a single pathway, ending at a CO2 cloud labeled '30 ATP' in TEAL; simple linear flow, nothing branches
production_viz.note: establish the naive expectation — complete oxidation = maximum energy

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] quiescent cell: glucose flows all the way to CO2 and 30 ATP

beats.narration_text: A dividing cell has a different primary need. It is not just powering itself — it is building a second copy. New membranes, a fresh genome, new proteins. All of that requires raw material. Carbon. The same carbon that is in glucose.

props.headline: So what does a dividing cell actually need? It is not…

beats.scene_description: a stylized cross-section of a cell mid-division, two daughter cells beginning to separate, rendered in the flat editorial collage style — cream ground, charcoal outlines; slow push-in toward the dividing interface
beats.image_prompt: B05, editorial illustration of an animal cell undergoing division, two daughter cells separating, simplified flat cross-section diagram, charcoal-ink line art on aged newsprint, desaturated muted tones, no color gradients, no text, biological diagram aesthetic
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL ai] dividing cell — the building requirement

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL ai] dividing cell — the building requirement

beats.narration_text: Complete oxidation is carbon waste for a cell that needs to double its mass. Every carbon atom that leaves as carbon dioxide is a carbon atom unavailable for building. Burning glucose all the way to CO2 is the wrong strategy when the cell's job is to divide.

production_viz.label: complete oxidation = all carbon lost as CO2
production_viz.mechanic: glucose node with six carbon atoms (six small squares); a single arrow flows all of them rightward into a CO2 cloud; the CO2 cloud is CRIMSON — everything gone, nothing to build with; a CRIMSON label 'nothing left to build with' appears
production_viz.note: the insight: CO2 = lost carbon. Crimson = the bad outcome for a builder.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] 6 carbons enter, 6 leave as CO2 — nothing left to build

beats.narration_text: The cancer cell runs glycolysis but routes carbon at the branch points where biosynthesis begins rather than sending it to the mitochondrion for full oxidation. Some carbon goes to ribose for new DNA. Some to serine for one-carbon reactions. Some becomes membrane lipid precursors.

production_viz.label: carbon fate split — the Manim move
production_viz.mechanic: glucose node on the left; glycolytic trunk extends rightward as a thick TEAL bar; three branches fan out upward and downward — nucleotides (TEAL, up-right), serine/one-carbon (TEAL, right), membrane lipids (TEAL, down-right); the final bottom branch is lactate (CRIMSON, down); this is the SPLIT manim move
production_viz.note: core visual of the film — carbon fans into destinations instead of flowing to CO2

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] carbon fate split — glycolytic trunk branching into nucleotides, serine, lipids, lactate

beats.narration_text: The excreted lactate is excess carbon the cell did not need for building — dumped to keep the chemistry balanced. Otto Warburg saw this lactate production in the 1920s and concluded the mitochondria were broken. They are not. The lactate is not a failure. It is a deliberate choice.

card.copy: The lactate is not a failure. It is a choice.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] pivot statement — the Warburg reframe

beats.narration_text: The cell is using glucose as a carbon source for biosynthesis, not as an energy source. The apparent ATP inefficiency is the cost of that carbon-allocation strategy. The cell compensates for low glycolytic ATP yield by burning other fuels — glutamine, fatty acids — in its mitochondria.

production_viz.label: two parallel metabolic streams
production_viz.mechanic: two horizontal tracks stacked; top track: glucose arrow split into biosynthetic branches (TEAL) with label 'carbon for building'; bottom track: glutamine/fatty acids arrow flowing into mitochondrion box, then ATP output (TEAL) with label 'energy from other fuels'; both tracks run in parallel, neither replaces the other
production_viz.note: establish that the cancer cell is NOT energy-starved — it has two streams

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two parallel streams — glucose for carbon, other fuels for energy

beats.narration_text: This is why PET scanning works. The cancer cell imports glucose voraciously — not to burn for energy but to use as raw material. The radioactive glucose analog enters on the same transporter, gets trapped by the same enzyme, and accumulates. The scanner detects it against a quiet background.

props.headline: This is why the PET scan works. The cancer cell needs…

beats.scene_description: editorial illustration of a PET scan image — two lymph nodes blazing bright against a dark background, rendered as a flat graphic showing the anatomical cross-section; newsprint treatment, muted except for the bright focal spots; slow pull-back reveal
beats.image_prompt: B10, editorial-style flat graphic of a PET scan cross-section image, two small lymph nodes glowing bright against a dark anatomical background, simplified medical illustration aesthetic, charcoal line-art body outline, two hot-spots circled, newsprint collage treatment, no text, no color gradients
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL ai] PET scan — the clinical implication

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL ai] PET scan — the clinical implication

beats.narration_text: A tumor lighting up on PET has chosen carbon allocation over energy efficiency — consuming glucose fast enough that radioactive tracer accumulates before it can exit. The bright spot is a metabolic strategy made visible.

production_viz.label: FDG trap — why the scanner sees it
production_viz.mechanic: a simple two-box diagram: left box 'cancer cell' shows a TEAL upward arrow for GLUT1 pulling glucose in, then a downward branch — one TEAL branch labeled 'building' and one CRIMSON arrow for lactate out; a separate TEAL glowing dot labeled 'FDG trapped' sits inside the cell box; right side shows a scanner signal icon (concentric arcs) in TEAL
production_viz.note: HIF-1alpha/VHL excluded per card; this is purely mechanistic consequence — high uptake, FDG trapped, signal emitted

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] FDG trap mechanism — glucose in, building + lactate out, tracer glows

beats.narration_text: One glucose molecule in a cancer cell doubling every twenty-four hours. Six carbons enter. Two fan into ribose — DNA synthesis requires them. Two go to serine — one-carbon metabolism. Six become acetyl-CoA — membrane lipids for the new cell. The rest exits as lactate. Six carbons in, six carbons allocated to structure.

production_viz.label: one glucose traced — the example
production_viz.mechanic: six small TEAL squares enter from the left representing the six carbons of glucose; they split along three labeled TEAL branches — '2 to ribose (DNA)' branching up-right, '2 to serine (one-carbon)' branching right, '6 to acetyl-CoA (membranes)' branching down-right (note: illustrative allocation, carbons shared across routes); a CRIMSON branch labeled 'rest exits as lactate' goes straight down; a chip labeled 'illustrative' sits in the corner
production_viz.note: ILLUSTRATIVE numbers per card's example seed — label as illustrative. Exclusion check: no TCA cycle, no enzymes named, no isotope tracing.

viz.note: illustrative carbon allocation — numbers are pedagogical, not precise measurements

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] one glucose traced across four destinations — the worked example

beats.narration_text: Compare: complete oxidation. Six carbons in, six leave as CO2. Thirty ATP generated — nothing left to build. A cell that fully burns its glucose cannot double its mass. It maintains. It does not divide. The cancer cell is not running an inefficient engine. It is running the right engine for the job.

production_viz.label: compare: carbon allocation vs complete oxidation
production_viz.mechanic: two side-by-side panels; left panel 'Dividing cell': six TEAL squares fan into three destination labels, a CRIMSON square fans to lactate — structure built, cell doubles; right panel 'Complete oxidation': six squares flow into one CO2 cloud (CRIMSON) with a TEAL '30 ATP' tag — but a CRIMSON label 'nothing to build with'; a bracket under right panel: 'maintains, does not divide'
production_viz.note: illustrative comparison — the narrative payoff of the example

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] side-by-side comparison — division strategy vs complete oxidation

beats.narration_text: Two ATP from glucose, not thirty — because the cell is optimizing for carbon, not energy. Run glycolysis fast, keep carbon at the biosynthetic branch points, burn other fuels for the energy budget. The Warburg effect is not a malfunction. It is the metabolic logic of a cell that needs to become two cells.

card.copy: 2 ATP, not 30 — because cancer cells optimize for carbon, not energy.
card.sub: the Warburg effect is a carbon-allocation strategy

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — question answered, one line
